category_title: Students with MLD using atypical multiples-list strategies for multiplication
scenario_title: Missing operand problem using atypical list access
minimal_context: Emily and her tutor are working on a missing operand multiplication problem during a one-on-one weekly tutoring session.

dialogue.speaker: tutor
dialogue.message: Let's look at this problem. What times 2 is 16?

dialogue.speaker: student
dialogue.message: You can’t do it… cause 2 doesn’t go into 16, evenly.

dialogue.speaker: tutor
dialogue.message: Take your time and try to work it out.

dialogue_combined:
TUTOR: Let's look at this problem. What times 2 is 16?

STUDENT: You can’t do it… cause 2 doesn’t go into 16, evenly.

TUTOR: Take your time and try to work it out.

STUDENT: Yeah. (Raises one finger) 5, (raises another finger) 10.

TUTOR: Keep going.

STUDENT: (Continues tracking on fingers) 6, 12. 7, 14. Oh! 8. Duh!

challenging_behavior_trigger.condition: Attempting to solve the problem 'what times 2 is 16?'
challenging_behavior_trigger.observable_behavior: Rather than iterating through her twos list, which was the given operand in the problem, she opted to access the second number on her fives, sixes, sevens, and eights lists.
related_quotes: You can’t do it… cause 2 doesn’t go into 16, evenly.

behavior_patterns.observable_behavior: recites a list of multiples while keeping track of her ordinal position in the list on her fingers
related_quotes: You like count up 7, (raises 1 finger) 14 (raises another finger), 21 (raises another finger)
